comment_text.sn17.13-20:1.5: The Purāṇas list the nikkha (“ingot”) as the largest gold coin, but here a siṅgīnikkha must be much more than a hundred of the largest coins, and hence cannot be a coin at all. Siṅgīsuvaṇṇa means “mountain gold” (AN 3.70:38.3). Given that the next step is the entire earth, perhaps siṅgīnikkha means “golden mountain”. A “golden mountain” (pabbatassa suvaṇṇassa) appears in a similar context at SN 4.20:3.2.

root_text.sn17.13-20:1.2: “Idhāhaṁ, bhikkhave, ekaccaṁ puggalaṁ evaṁ cetasā ceto paricca pajānāmi:
root_text.sn17.13-20:1.11: Tamenaṁ passāmi aparena samayena lābhasakkārasilokena abhibhūtaṁ pariyādiṇṇacittaṁ sampajānamusā bhāsantaṁ.

translation_text.sn17.13-20:1.2: “Mendicants, when I’ve comprehended the mind of a certain individual, I understand:
translation_text.sn17.13-20:1.3: ‘This venerable would not tell a deliberate lie even for the sake of a gold ingot.’ …”
translation_text.sn17.13-20:1.4: “‘… for the sake of a hundred gold ingots.’ …”
translation_text.sn17.13-20:1.5: “‘… for the sake of a mountain of gold.’ …”
translation_text.sn17.13-20:1.6: “‘… for the sake of a hundred mountains of gold.’ …”
translation_text.sn17.13-20:1.7: “‘… for the sake of the whole earth full of gold.’ …”
translation_text.sn17.13-20:1.8: “‘… for any kind of material reward.’ …”
translation_text.sn17.13-20:1.10: “‘… for the sake of the finest lady in the land.’
translation_text.sn17.13-20:1.11: But some time later I see them tell a deliberate lie because their mind is overcome and overwhelmed by possessions, honor, and popularity.
translation_text.sn17.13-20:1.12: So grim are possessions, honor, and popularity. …”
